perturbation.original_text: During the period from the date of this Agreement and the completion of the Squeeze-out (the "Restricted Period"), except as contemplated by this Agreement, required by applicable Law or otherwise agreed to in writing by Acquiror, the Company shall operate in the ordinary course of business consistent with the past practice and use its reasonable efforts to preserve intact the material components of its current business organization, including keeping available the services of current officers and key employees, and use its reasonable efforts to maintain its relations and good will with all material suppliers, material customers, governmental bodies and other material business relations intact its business relationships.
perturbation.changed_text: During the period from the date of this Agreement and the completion of the Squeeze-out (the "Restricted Period"), except as contemplated by this Agreement, required by applicable Law or otherwise agreed to in writing by Acquiror, the Company should endeavor to operate in a manner generally consistent with past practice and make efforts, as it deems commercially reasonable, to maintain its business organization and relationships.
perturbation.explanation: The modification weakens the obligation to operate in the ordinary course of business and maintain relationships. Replacing 'shall operate' with 'should endeavor to operate,' 'reasonable efforts' with 'efforts, as it deems commercially reasonable' introduces ambiguity and makes the obligation less strict. This allows the Company more discretion which may not be in line with requirements.
perturbation.law_citation: Article 355 of the Japanese Companies Act
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 355 of the Japanese Companies Act imposes a duty of due care on directors, requiring them to exercise the care of a good manager. The modified text, by using softer language like 'should endeavor' and introducing commercial reasonableness as a qualifier, potentially contradicts this duty by allowing directors to prioritize commercial interests over the company's well-being. It could make it harder to prove a breach of fiduciary duty if the directors make decisions that harm the company but are arguably commercially reasonable, even if they fail to act in good faith. The original, stronger language would be more enforceable in holding directors accountable under Article 355.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall operate' and 'reasonable efforts' to 'should endeavor' and 'efforts, as it deems commercially reasonable' weakens the original obligation. The Law Explanation argues that this contradicts Article 355 of the Japanese Companies Act, which imposes a duty of due care on directors. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing, and Snippet 2 only points to a Japanese law search engine, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation provide enough context to suggest that the modified text introduces ambiguity that could allow directors to prioritize commercial interests over their duty of care, potentially making it harder to prove a breach of fiduciary duty.

perturbation.original_text: Subject to the goals, parameters and integration activities outlined in the integration plan, SPI shall (i) provide the employees of the Company with employee incentives under such terms and conditions as not less favorable (taking into account, among other things, tax implications) to the incentives made available by the Company to such its employees as of the date of this Agreement […***…], and thereafter under such terms and conditions as not less favorable (taking into account, among other things, tax implications, local laws, and SPI's practices with respect to the employees of Acquiror) to those of the incentives made available by SPI to its employees, and (ii) […***…] the […***…] of the […***… ] of the […***…] as of the […***…] of this [… ***…] at […***…] the […***…] of the […***…] of the […***…] on […***…].
perturbation.changed_text: Subject to the goals, parameters and integration activities outlined in the integration plan, SPI should, to the extent reasonably practicable, aim to provide the employees of the Company with employee incentives that are generally comparable to the incentives made available by the Company as of the date of this Agreement. The details of such incentives will be determined at SPI's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: This modification weakens the commitment to providing comparable employee incentives. Replacing 'shall provide' with 'should, to the extent reasonably practicable, aim to provide' introduces ambiguity and makes the obligation less binding. Adding 'generally comparable' makes the standard subjective, and granting SPI full 'discretion' effectively removes any real obligation. This could contradict labor laws regarding fair treatment after a business transfer, depending on how it is interpreted and implemented.
perturbation.law_citation: Article 4 of the Labor Standards Act
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 4 of the Labor Standards Act prohibits discrimination in working conditions based on nationality, creed, or social status. While it doesn't directly mandate specific incentives, drastically altering incentives post-acquisition, particularly if it disproportionately affects former Company employees, could be construed as indirect discrimination or a failure to maintain fair working conditions. The modified text, with its ambiguous 'generally comparable' and SPI's sole 'discretion,' provides a loophole that could allow SPI to implement discriminatory practices under the guise of business integration. The original text, while still redacted, at least suggests a commitment to 'not less favorable' terms which gives a clear reference point.
perturbation.justification: The original contract text mandates that SPI "shall provide" employee incentives under conditions "not less favorable". The changed text weakens this obligation to 'should, to the extent reasonably practicable, aim to provide' incentives that are 'generally comparable'. This introduces ambiguity and discretion ('details...determined at SPI's discretion'), undermining the original commitment. Although the scraped law snippets are not helpful, the perturbation explanation, the LLM's law explanation and the comparison of the original and changed text strongly suggests a legal contradiction was created.
